proposal.title: A Multi-State Nested Case-Control Study of Cumulative Paraquat Exposure and Incident Parkinson's Disease, with Biomarker Calibration
proposal.study_type: Nested Case-Control Study within a Prospective Cohort
proposal.hypothesis: H0: Cumulative residential and occupational paraquat exposure is not associated with incident Parkinson's disease (ICD-10 G20) after adjustment for co-exposures and demographic confounders. H1: Cumulative paraquat exposure is associated with increased incidence of PD, with a positive dose-response relationship and effect modification by dopamine transporter and mitochondrial-function genotypes.
proposal.population: Adults aged 45-80 at enrollment residing in counties with ≥10th-percentile USGS-reported paraquat application during 1995-2015, across five agricultural states (California, Iowa, North Carolina, Minnesota, Florida). Inclusion: ≥5 years of residential history in a qualifying county, consent to records linkage. Exclusion: prior PD diagnosis, prior atypical parkinsonism, known monogenic PD mutation on pre-enrollment screening.
proposal.sample_size_rationale: Assuming an expected PD incidence of 0.3% over 10 years and a minimum detectable OR of 1.5 for high vs. low cumulative paraquat exposure (upper-vs-lower-tertile contrast), with alpha=0.05 and power=0.80, 480 incident PD cases and 1,920 matched controls (1:4 matching on age, sex, county, enrollment year) are required. Total cohort size of approximately 160,000 at enrollment provides the yield for this case count.
proposal.exposure_assessment: Three-tier exposure assessment. Tier 1 (all participants): modeled cumulative paraquat exposure from residential history linked to USGS county-year pesticide-use files and crop-level application rasters. Tier 2 (occupational subset): job-exposure matrix combining self-reported agricultural work history with pesticide-applicator licensing records. Tier 3 (biomarker subset, n=2,400): urinary paraquat and dialkyl-phosphate metabolite measurements at enrollment and year 5, stratified across exposure tertiles to calibrate Tier 1 and Tier 2 estimates. Co-exposure to maneb, rotenone, chlorpyrifos, and ziram quantified identically.
proposal.outcome_assessment: Incident PD defined per MDS-UPDRS criteria, ascertained via (a) annual health questionnaire screening items, (b) linkage to Medicare and state All-Payer Claims Database for ICD-10 G20 diagnoses, and (c) neurologist adjudication of a random 10% sample of claims-identified cases plus all questionnaire-flagged cases. Death-certificate PD (underlying or contributing cause) serves as a secondary outcome for sensitivity analysis.
confounders: ALDH2 and DAT genotype (in genotyped subset)
proposal.analysis_plan: Primary analysis: conditional logistic regression of incident PD on tertiles of cumulative paraquat exposure, conditioning on matching variables and adjusting for demographic and co-exposure confounders above. Dose-response assessed via restricted cubic splines and tests for linear trend. Secondary analyses: (1) effect modification by DAT and ALDH2 genotype in the genotyped subset, tested via multiplicative interaction terms; (2) multi-pesticide joint models (quantile g-computation and WQS regression) to isolate paraquat's independent contribution from the broader pesticide mixture; (3) biomarker-calibrated sensitivity analysis using regression-calibration with the Tier 3 subset; (4) spatial heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors via conditional autoregressive models at the county level. Missing data handled via multiple imputation (m=20). Pre-registered on OSF before data analysis begins.
limitations: Residual confounding by unmeasured co-exposures to other neurotoxic pesticides or industrial chemicals not captured by the study.
Exposure misclassification from modeled (rather than measured) paraquat application at the residence level; partially mitigated by the biomarker calibration subset.
Long latency between exposure and PD onset means some exposure windows (e.g., occupational exposures from the 1970s-1980s) will be reconstructed from imperfect historical records.
PD diagnosis in claims data has known sensitivity/specificity limitations; neurologist adjudication mitigates but does not eliminate this bias.
Selection into agricultural residence or occupation may correlate with unmeasured lifestyle factors (e.g., rural diet, pesticide-related protective gear use).
Generalizability limited to the five participating states and to the age range studied; urban paraquat exposure (household or ambient drift) is not directly captured.
proposal.ethical_considerations: IRB approval required at each participating site and the coordinating center. Informed consent covers (a) records linkage to Medicare/claims/state registries, (b) optional biomarker sub-study participation, (c) optional genotyping. Genotype results are not returned to participants individually unless they meet pre-specified medically actionable criteria. Data stored in a HIPAA-compliant enclave with differential-privacy protections for public use files. Equity consideration: oversample historically underrepresented agricultural-worker communities (including migrant workers) via partnership with community health centers, with Spanish-language and low-literacy consent materials.
proposal.timeline: Year 1: finalize protocol, IRB approvals, site activation, recruitment infrastructure. Years 2-3: enrollment (n=160,000) and baseline data collection. Years 4-8: prospective follow-up with annual questionnaires and biennial claims linkage refresh. Year 5: Tier-3 biomarker sub-study enrollment and assays. Years 9-10: case ascertainment adjudication, final analysis, pre-registered primary analysis, sensitivity analyses, manuscript preparation, data release.
proposal.resources: Personnel: Principal Investigator (30% FTE), 2 Co-Is (20% FTE each), study coordinator at each of 5 sites, 3 biostatisticians, 2 exposure-science methodologists, 1 neurologist adjudicator panel (6 members, 5% FTE each), data manager, 2 research assistants per site, community liaison at each site. Equipment: secure data enclave, mass-spectrometry core contract for paraquat metabolite assays, genotyping array contract. Budget estimate: $18-22M over 10 years. Funding: NIEHS (primary), NINDS (biomarker supplement), NIOSH (occupational subset), foundation co-funding for equity/community-engagement components.

gap_resolutions.uncertainty: Co-exposure confounding: paraquat tracks with maneb, rotenone, and other pesticides at the county level; isolating paraquat's independent contribution is not possible with the current dataset.
gap_resolutions.how_addressed: Quantify maneb, rotenone, chlorpyrifos, and ziram alongside paraquat in all three exposure tiers. Use quantile g-computation and WQS regression to estimate each compound's independent contribution to the joint mixture effect. Publish all co-exposure correlation matrices and mixture-model sensitivity analyses.

gap_resolutions.uncertainty: Ecological fallacy: individual-level risk cannot be inferred from county-level correlation; individual-level studies support but do not guarantee transportability.
gap_resolutions.how_addressed: Study design is individual-level from the outset. County-level modeled exposure is a covariate, not the unit of analysis. Conditional logistic regression with individual matching removes the ecological structure entirely.

gap_resolutions.uncertainty: Exposure misclassification: USGS pesticide-use estimates are modeled, not measured; individual biomarker exposure data would substantially tighten inference.
gap_resolutions.how_addressed: Tier-3 biomarker sub-study (n=2,400) provides gold-standard urinary paraquat metabolite measurements. Regression-calibration transports the biomarker-measured exposure-outcome relationship to the full cohort. Validation of modeled vs. measured exposure is a pre-specified secondary aim.

alternative_designs.study_type: Retrospective Cohort via Medicare Claims Linkage
alternative_designs.description: Link 20-year Medicare claims history to historical USGS paraquat application data by beneficiary ZIP; ascertain incident PD from claims.
strengths: Much lower cost (~$2-3M) and faster completion (~3-4 years).
Larger effective sample size (millions of beneficiaries).
weaknesses: Residential-history granularity limited to ZIP-year pairs available in Medicare.
Unmeasured confounding by lifestyle factors not captured in claims.
PD diagnosis in claims has ~70% PPV without adjudication.

alternative_designs.study_type: Case-Crossover Study within Existing Agricultural Cohorts
alternative_designs.description: Within the Agricultural Health Study or similar active cohort, compare each participant's exposure in the 5-10 years prior to PD onset with their own exposure in earlier reference periods.
strengths: Controls for all time-invariant individual confounders by design (each subject is their own control).
Leverages existing exposure infrastructure — no new cohort needed.
weaknesses: Requires time-varying exposure with sufficient within-person variability.
Long PD latency complicates choice of reference windows.
Underpowered for rare gene-environment interaction subgroups.

alternative_designs.description: Use genetic variants affecting paraquat clearance (e.g., ALDH2, DAT) as instrumental variables for chronic paraquat exposure in large biobank samples (UK Biobank, All of Us).
strengths: Robust to many forms of confounding by design.
Exploits existing biobank infrastructure — very low marginal cost.
weaknesses: Valid instruments for paraquat-specific exposure are weak or absent; most candidate variants affect broader xenobiotic metabolism.
Assumes linear gene-exposure and exposure-outcome relationships.
Biobank participants are non-representative and underpowered for PD incidence.

proposal.disclaimer: Generated proposal — requires expert review and institutional adaptation before submission.

markdown:
# A Multi-State Nested Case-Control Study of Cumulative Paraquat Exposure and Incident Parkinson's Disease, with Biomarker Calibration

**Study Type:** Nested Case-Control Study within a Prospective Cohort

## Hypothesis

H0: Cumulative residential and occupational paraquat exposure is not associated with incident Parkinson's disease (ICD-10 G20) after adjustment for co-exposures and demographic confounders. H1: Cumulative paraquat exposure is associated with increased incidence of PD, with a positive dose-response relationship and effect modification by dopamine transporter and mitochondrial-function genotypes.

## Study Population

Adults aged 45-80 at enrollment residing in counties with ≥10th-percentile USGS-reported paraquat application during 1995-2015, across five agricultural states (California, Iowa, North Carolina, Minnesota, Florida). Inclusion: ≥5 years of residential history in a qualifying county, consent to records linkage. Exclusion: prior PD diagnosis, prior atypical parkinsonism, known monogenic PD mutation on pre-enrollment screening.

## Sample Size Rationale

Assuming an expected PD incidence of 0.3% over 10 years and a minimum detectable OR of 1.5 for high vs. low cumulative paraquat exposure (upper-vs-lower-tertile contrast), with alpha=0.05 and power=0.80, 480 incident PD cases and 1,920 matched controls (1:4 matching on age, sex, county, enrollment year) are required. Total cohort size of approximately 160,000 at enrollment provides the yield for this case count.

## Exposure and Outcome Assessment

**Exposure assessment:** Three-tier exposure assessment. Tier 1 (all participants): modeled cumulative paraquat exposure from residential history linked to USGS county-year pesticide-use files and crop-level application rasters. Tier 2 (occupational subset): job-exposure matrix combining self-reported agricultural work history with pesticide-applicator licensing records. Tier 3 (biomarker subset, n=2,400): urinary paraquat and dialkyl-phosphate metabolite measurements at enrollment and year 5, stratified across exposure tertiles to calibrate Tier 1 and Tier 2 estimates. Co-exposure to maneb, rotenone, chlorpyrifos, and ziram quantified identically.

**Outcome assessment:** Incident PD defined per MDS-UPDRS criteria, ascertained via (a) annual health questionnaire screening items, (b) linkage to Medicare and state All-Payer Claims Database for ICD-10 G20 diagnoses, and (c) neurologist adjudication of a random 10% sample of claims-identified cases plus all questionnaire-flagged cases. Death-certificate PD (underlying or contributing cause) serves as a secondary outcome for sensitivity analysis.

## Confounders to Control

- Age (continuous)
- Sex
- Race / ethnicity (self-reported)
- Educational attainment
- Household income at enrollment
- Rural / urban residence (RUCC)
- Occupational farming status and duration
- Head trauma history (self-report + claims)
- Smoking status and pack-years
- Well-water consumption
- Co-exposure: maneb kg/km² cumulative
- Co-exposure: rotenone kg/km² cumulative
- Co-exposure: chlorpyrifos kg/km² cumulative
- Healthcare-access index (county-level PCP ratio)
- ALDH2 and DAT genotype (in genotyped subset)

## Analysis Plan

Primary analysis: conditional logistic regression of incident PD on tertiles of cumulative paraquat exposure, conditioning on matching variables and adjusting for demographic and co-exposure confounders above. Dose-response assessed via restricted cubic splines and tests for linear trend. Secondary analyses: (1) effect modification by DAT and ALDH2 genotype in the genotyped subset, tested via multiplicative interaction terms; (2) multi-pesticide joint models (quantile g-computation and WQS regression) to isolate paraquat's independent contribution from the broader pesticide mixture; (3) biomarker-calibrated sensitivity analysis using regression-calibration with the Tier 3 subset; (4) spatial heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors via conditional autoregressive models at the county level. Missing data handled via multiple imputation (m=20). Pre-registered on OSF before data analysis begins.

## Limitations

- Residual confounding by unmeasured co-exposures to other neurotoxic pesticides or industrial chemicals not captured by the study.
- Exposure misclassification from modeled (rather than measured) paraquat application at the residence level; partially mitigated by the biomarker calibration subset.
- Long latency between exposure and PD onset means some exposure windows (e.g., occupational exposures from the 1970s-1980s) will be reconstructed from imperfect historical records.
- PD diagnosis in claims data has known sensitivity/specificity limitations; neurologist adjudication mitigates but does not eliminate this bias.
- Selection into agricultural residence or occupation may correlate with unmeasured lifestyle factors (e.g., rural diet, pesticide-related protective gear use).
- Generalizability limited to the five participating states and to the age range studied; urban paraquat exposure (household or ambient drift) is not directly captured.

## Ethical Considerations

IRB approval required at each participating site and the coordinating center. Informed consent covers (a) records linkage to Medicare/claims/state registries, (b) optional biomarker sub-study participation, (c) optional genotyping. Genotype results are not returned to participants individually unless they meet pre-specified medically actionable criteria. Data stored in a HIPAA-compliant enclave with differential-privacy protections for public use files. Equity consideration: oversample historically underrepresented agricultural-worker communities (including migrant workers) via partnership with community health centers, with Spanish-language and low-literacy consent materials.

## Timeline

Year 1: finalize protocol, IRB approvals, site activation, recruitment infrastructure. Years 2-3: enrollment (n=160,000) and baseline data collection. Years 4-8: prospective follow-up with annual questionnaires and biennial claims linkage refresh. Year 5: Tier-3 biomarker sub-study enrollment and assays. Years 9-10: case ascertainment adjudication, final analysis, pre-registered primary analysis, sensitivity analyses, manuscript preparation, data release.

## Resources

Personnel: Principal Investigator (30% FTE), 2 Co-Is (20% FTE each), study coordinator at each of 5 sites, 3 biostatisticians, 2 exposure-science methodologists, 1 neurologist adjudicator panel (6 members, 5% FTE each), data manager, 2 research assistants per site, community liaison at each site. Equipment: secure data enclave, mass-spectrometry core contract for paraquat metabolite assays, genotyping array contract. Budget estimate: $18-22M over 10 years. Funding: NIEHS (primary), NINDS (biomarker supplement), NIOSH (occupational subset), foundation co-funding for equity/community-engagement components.

## Gap Resolution

| Uncertainty | How Addressed |
|-------------|---------------|
| Co-exposure confounding: paraquat tracks with maneb, rotenone, and other pesticides at the county level; isolating paraquat's independent contribution is not possible with the current dataset. | Quantify maneb, rotenone, chlorpyrifos, and ziram alongside paraquat in all three exposure tiers. Use quantile g-computation and WQS regression to estimate each compound's independent contribution to the joint mixture effect. Publish all co-exposure correlation matrices and mixture-model sensitivity analyses. |
| Ecological fallacy: individual-level risk cannot be inferred from county-level correlation; individual-level studies support but do not guarantee transportability. | Study design is individual-level from the outset. County-level modeled exposure is a covariate, not the unit of analysis. Conditional logistic regression with individual matching removes the ecological structure entirely. |
| Exposure misclassification: USGS pesticide-use estimates are modeled, not measured; individual biomarker exposure data would substantially tighten inference. | Tier-3 biomarker sub-study (n=2,400) provides gold-standard urinary paraquat metabolite measurements. Regression-calibration transports the biomarker-measured exposure-outcome relationship to the full cohort. Validation of modeled vs. measured exposure is a pre-specified secondary aim. |

## Alternative Study Designs

| Study Type | Description | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|------------|-------------|-----------|------------|
| Retrospective Cohort via Medicare Claims Linkage | Link 20-year Medicare claims history to historical USGS paraquat application data by beneficiary ZIP; ascertain incident PD from claims. | Much lower cost (~$2-3M) and faster completion (~3-4 years).; Larger effective sample size (millions of beneficiaries).; Minimal recruitment bias. | No biomarker calibration possible retrospectively.; Residential-history granularity limited to ZIP-year pairs available in Medicare.; Unmeasured confounding by lifestyle factors not captured in claims.; PD diagnosis in claims has ~70% PPV without adjudication. |
| Case-Crossover Study within Existing Agricultural Cohorts | Within the Agricultural Health Study or similar active cohort, compare each participant's exposure in the 5-10 years prior to PD onset with their own exposure in earlier reference periods. | Controls for all time-invariant individual confounders by design (each subject is their own control).; Leverages existing exposure infrastructure — no new cohort needed.; Small incremental budget (~$500K-1M). | Requires time-varying exposure with sufficient within-person variability.; Long PD latency complicates choice of reference windows.; Cohort composition (licensed applicators) limits generalizability.; Underpowered for rare gene-environment interaction subgroups. |
| Mendelian Randomization Using Paraquat-Metabolism Variants | Use genetic variants affecting paraquat clearance (e.g., ALDH2, DAT) as instrumental variables for chronic paraquat exposure in large biobank samples (UK Biobank, All of Us). | Circumvents most exposure-measurement problems.; Robust to many forms of confounding by design.; Exploits existing biobank infrastructure — very low marginal cost. | Valid instruments for paraquat-specific exposure are weak or absent; most candidate variants affect broader xenobiotic metabolism.; Assumes linear gene-exposure and exposure-outcome relationships.; Biobank participants are non-representative and underpowered for PD incidence. |

---

**Generated proposal — requires expert review and institutional adaptation before submission.**
